title: Pappardelle ai porcini
subtitle: Pappardelle with porcini and parsley in olive oil
excerpt: Wide pappardelle ribbons tossed with reconstituted porcini, garlic, and parsley in olive oil — no cream, no cheese in the sauce. The Tuscan version of the porcini pasta, drier and more intense than the cream variant, the cooking liquid reduced to a concentrated glaze.
sourceNotes: Based on the Tuscan pasta ai porcini tradition documented in Giuliano Bugialli, 'The Fine Art of Italian Cooking' (1977) and Marcella Hazan, 'Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking' (1992). The olive oil version is the older preparation; the cream variant is a more recent adaptation.

content.text: Where the Emilian version of porcini pasta uses cream, this Tuscan preparation uses olive oil and the mushroom's own concentrated soaking liquid. The result is a more austere dish — darker, more intensely flavoured, with the porcini character unmediated by dairy. The porcini soaking liquid reduces to almost nothing in the pan, leaving a dark coating on the mushrooms before the pasta water loosens it into a coating sauce.

Pappardelle is wide enough to hold the sauce and substantial enough to stand alongside the bold mushroom flavour. Fresh pappardelle, if available, cooks in 2–3 minutes and has a softer texture; dried pappardelle takes longer but handles the tossing better without breaking.

items.prepNote: soaked in 300 ml warm water for 20 minutes

content.text: Soak {{dried-porcini}} of dried porcini in 300 ml of warm water for 20 minutes. Lift out, squeeze gently, and chop roughly. Strain the soaking liquid carefully through a fine sieve, leaving the last tablespoon in the bowl.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over a medium heat. Add {{garlic}} sliced garlic and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes. When the garlic begins to colour, add the mushrooms and cook for 3 minutes. Pour in the strained porcini liquid. Simmer until reduced by two-thirds — the liquid should be almost gone and the mushrooms glazed. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt.

Cook {{pappardelle}} of pappardelle in well-salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve a large mug of pasta water, then drain. Add the pasta to the mushroom pan over a medium heat with 4–5 tablespoons of pasta water. Toss vigorously to coat. Add most of the {{parsley}} chopped parsley and {{black-pepper-ground}} of black pepper. Toss once more. Serve with the remaining parsley and {{parmesan}} parmesan at the table.

items.fix: Add pasta water a tablespoon at a time — the starch emulsifies with the olive oil and coats the pasta rather than making it wet.

items.cause: Heat too high when the liquid was added
items.fix: Reduce heat when the liquid goes in. It should simmer, not boil away in 30 seconds.

content.text: Porcini pasta in olive oil is the traditional Tuscan preparation, found in the hill towns south of Florence where the forests yield porcini from late summer. The cream version associated with Emilia came later and is now more widely known outside Italy. Giuliano Bugialli, whose 'Fine Art of Italian Cooking' documented Tuscan home cooking in the 1970s, describes the olive oil preparation as the original and notes that the cream addition was an embellishment of the postwar period.
